title: Wet-felted shopping bag
subtitle: A sturdy merino tote, approximately 35 × 30 cm, wet-felted over a foam resist with cord handles
excerpt: A thick-walled merino shopping bag, approximately 35 × 30 cm, formed over a foam board resist. Six layers of roving give walls dense enough to carry 2-3 kg. Twisted merino cord handles are attached after fulling and drying.
sourceNotes: Wet-felted bag making is a standard intermediate resist project. Method synthesised from general flat-resist wet-felting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stiff form placed inside roving layers to prevent fibres bonding across the opening of a hollow object.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, the raw material for wet felting.
Vigorous agitation after initial felting to compress and shrink the fabric to its finished density.
The reduction in dimensions that occurs during wet felting. Merino typically shrinks 30% in each direction during full processing.

content.text: A wet-felted shopping bag is built over a flat foam-board
resist
cut to match the desired finished size, scaled up to account for
take-up
. Six cross-hatch layers of
roving
per side give a wall thickness of approximately 6-8 mm after
fulling
. The bag can carry moderate loads; for heavier use, the handles should be reinforced with a length of cotton webbing stitched inside.

items.name: Merino roving, contrasting colour (optional stripe or border)
Foam board or thick polystyrene, cut to 50 × 43 cm (resist)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut the resist
For a finished bag of 35 × 30 cm, cut the resist to 50 × 43 cm to allow 30% shrinkage. Cover both sides of the resist in plastic to prevent the felt from bonding to it.
Step 2: Lay six layers per side

Lay three horizontal and three vertical layers of roving on the first side of the resist, extending 3 cm beyond all edges. Fold the overhang tightly over the edges. Flip the resist and lay six layers on the second side. Fold the remaining overhangs. The roving should be evenly thick, approximately 2-3 cm deep before wetting. For a striped design, lay contrasting colour roving in horizontal bands over the outermost layer before wetting.
Step 3: Wet and rub both sides

Saturate both sides with hot soapy water. Place the felting net over the first side and rub in small circular movements for 12 minutes per side. The piece should feel stable and firm. Roll in the bamboo mat and roll back and forth for 5 minutes per direction, rotating 90 degrees between each roll.
Step 4: Remove resist and full

Slide the resist out through the top opening. Continue rolling and throwing the bag against the mat for 20-30 minutes, re-wetting as needed, until the bag measures approximately 38 × 33 cm (it will continue to shrink as it dries). Trim the top opening to a straight line.
Step 5: Make and attach handles

Twist two lengths of roving into cords approximately 55 cm long each (they will shrink slightly when felted). Pre-felt each cord by rubbing it between your palms with soapy water for 5 minutes, then rinse. When the bag is almost dry, mark two handle positions on each side, 8 cm apart and 3 cm from the top edge. Thread the handles through small holes made with a knitting needle and tie or sew the ends securely on the inside. Allow to dry completely.

items.problem: The bag bottom is thinner than the sides
items.cause: The bottom edge received less rubbing than the sides during processing
items.fix: Spend additional time working the bottom corners and base during the rolling stage. Roll the bag with the base facing outward for several passes.

items.problem: The bag opening has become too small to remove the resist
items.cause: Fulling progressed beyond the planned dimensions before removing the resist
items.fix: Wet the bag thoroughly and work a hand inside the opening, gradually stretching it outward while the felt is wet and warm. Re-stretch to dimensions after each wetting if needed.

items.problem: The handles pull away from the bag body under load
items.cause: The handle attachment was not reinforced through the full wall thickness
items.fix: Re-wet the handle join areas. Sew a small square of leather or thick wool felt to the inside of the bag at each handle, stitching through both the felt patch and the bag wall.

content.text: Care
Hand-wash in cool water with wool-safe detergent. Stuff the bag with a towel or newspaper while drying to maintain the shape. Do not machine wash.
